data.title: Sea: Market Fear Creates A Compelling Entry Point For An SEA Growth Giant
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Sea Limited (SE), I rated them a Hold due to the price seemingly pricing quite significant potential already while warning about the risks of not meeting the market’s expectations, and even if the price initially increased to nearly $200 since then, it quickly dropped to significantly more depressed levels, opening a potential opportunity.

Right now, I believe SE is worth upgrading to a Buy, with excellent financials paired with a solid valuation and significant long-term potential, while the rising geopolitical tensions around the US can even lead to more demand for high-quality SEA alternatives.

Internal Developments

As we can see, Sea’s Q3 delivered exceptional growth in almost everything, with the net income up 144.6%, EPS up 145.8%, and Adjusted EBITDA growth of 67.7%. This comes as their e-commerce revenue increased 36.6%, digital finance 60.8%, and digital entertainment went up 31.2% YoY, leading to a total revenue increase of 38.3% as the company continued its expansion while also turning profitable, being able to deliver economies of scale that are clearly visible in the bottom line while also investing significantly. About that, the company’s CFO also gave the following details during their Q3 Earnings Call:

In terms of where we are investing, one of the things is we mentioned in the opening, further investment into the logistic capabilities and fulfillment capabilities. And beyond that, we are also deepening our buy engagement and wallet shares through, for example, our Shopee VIP program that we shared in the opening as well. And all those efforts have been showing pretty good results. Our buy frequency improved 12% year-to-year and average monthly active buyers increased 15% year-to-year as well, which contributes to our great growth this quarter, which is way above the guidance we gave in the earlier of the year, which is 20%. Most of the investments are less fixed per se. We take a relatively asset-light approach even coming to our logistics and fulfillment businesses.

Financially, based on SE’s latest report, we continue to see an excellent position, with the current assets alone covering their total liabilities, although all their convertible notes are now current, although there’s plenty of cash (over $3 billion plus ~$6.8 billion in short-term investments) to cover these if they don’t want to refinance (note that $1.15 billion matured in December 2025, but 2026 notes also became current).

Still, this remains a fortress balance sheet, with significant financial flexibility and plenty of room for them to increase their leverage.

I'll also note that recently, Sea's CEO told employees that a $1 trillion valuation is possible thanks to their AI initiatives, although I'd like to see more action rather than words in this regard, as the market seems to be fed up with everyone talking about AI. It's true that there is potential in both e-commerce and gaming, but the market likely needs much more proof nowadays.

Meanwhile, Sea also announced a $1 billion buyback program, although that’s not really that meaningful given their $71.88 billion, but it at least shows some conviction in their valuation and potential.

Macro Developments

One key potential catalyst that I have to mention for foreign assets like this one is the potential “capital migration” away from the US, which can reignite the market’s demand for strong assets with non-US exposure.

Right now, the country is already struggling due to the high-rate environment and worries about the Fed’s next moves or even its independence (Powell’s term ends in May 2026), while countless recent studies highlight the significant pressure still felt by the regular consumer - especially lower-income.

Even the basic food companies are reporting issues as people continue trading down to discount stores or private-label or value-oriented brands, while tariffs add yet another layer of uncertainty.

This culminated recently with Trump announcing a new round of tariffs against allies that don’t agree with his plans for Greenland (the “complete and total purchase of Greenland”), with the EU currently preparing a response that would allow reciprocal tariffs through the Union’s anti-coercion tool, being the first time it would be used since it was enacted in 2023 and potentially even reverting the previous trade agreement.

Of course, this could then translate into even more inflation/rising costs for the average American, who was already reportedly under more stress at the end of 2025 compared to the beginning of the year, fueled by rising job insecurity, lower income, and overall higher costs of living.

All this rising uncertainty could eventually be the “last drop” for the market’s trust or risk appetite, and we’re already looking at a weak USD combined with high treasury yields despite 3 cuts in late 2025, as the market takes into account other macro factors than just what the Fed is doing - which, by the way, has been the most divided since 2019 back in December 2025.

On the Friday before this news came out (it was just a threat back then), we saw the 10-year treasuries jump to their highest level since September, which could be just the beginning of the market either pricing the new risks it has to take (thus increasing the required yield) or even the potential to see the market move into global assets in an effort to diversify away from these risks.

In this regard, Sea offers excellent exposure to SEA, being a very healthy company with a proven track record and significant growth potential still left, with extra potential if they actually manage to integrate AI, as the CEO said, while Garena can offer a solid and relatively predictable source of cash to fund this expansion.

Again, the region’s e-commerce potential is massive, with several studies expecting double-digit CAGR by 2030, while they use all this momentum to also develop their financial services on top, and I believe we shouldn’t ignore the possibility of some acquisitions given the company’s massive size.

However, as attractive as this region’s potential is - arguably being one of the most attractive in the long term, so far - the competition is just as intense. This is ultimately a significant risk for SE, as virtually all major players (especially from China) have some kind of presence or at least plan to develop in SEA, which can ultimately limit the company’s growth prospects in the long term.

Risks Reiterated

Of course, nothing guarantees that the market will move into assets like SE in the future, which can lead to a “regional tax” hurting their valuation for longer, while they’ll eventually have to face competition from the US, which can take advantage of better market yields alongside other giants in the market, especially from China.

Meanwhile, Garena can face declines as competition and device replacements provide better or more accessible options, hurting one of SE’s pillars for income, while e-commerce and financial services already face very significant competition.

Valuation

In SEA’s case, we'll have to estimate a proxy for the free cash flow for their DCF valuation. Based on the last four reports, we get to a TTM Adjusted EBITDA of ~$3.24 billion (even though the Q4'24 number could be ~$300 million lower than what it might be in Q4'25 if there's no seasonality), minus an estimated CAPEX of $662.9 million TTM and $530.7 million in taxes, with zero working capital changes to assume it's neutral plus $311.89 million in net interest income (annualizing their 9M’25 levels), getting to a proxy Unlevered FCF of roughly $2.36 billion in 2025.

To be a bit more conservative, let’s assume that this can grow at a CAGR of 17.5% over the first 5 years to take into account the significant expansion potential, fundamental growth of the region but also high competitive pressure, as well as a 12% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years since these trends would continue, with a relatively high 4% terminal growth rate given the region’s potential, as well as a WACC of 10% (~11% cost of equity, ~5.4% cost of debt), with a net cash position of $7.05 billion (including short-term investments) and 638.09 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This way, we get to an enterprise value of $94.02 billion, an equity value of $101.07 billion after adding the net cash, for an intrinsic value of $158.40 per share, which is above the current $121.42 by about 30%, while there is certainly more potential, especially if the market sees a higher “need” for non-US assets - after all, this nearly reached $200 a few months ago.

Conclusions

I’m upgrading Sea Limited to a Buy thanks to the excellent financial health and massive potential to grow, while the valuation dropped to attractive levels that can justify buying, backed by strong near- and long-term tailwinds.

There are certainly region-specific risks combined with fear that their growth can slow down - especially since they depend on discretionary budgets that can be affected even more if macro pressures advance - although the current price arguably offers a solid margin of safety.

data.title: Voya Balanced Large Cap Core Value SMA (Merrill) Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

A 55/45 multi-asset strategy providing exposure to the investment expertise of the Voya Investment Management equity and fixed income teams in a single strategy.

Key takeaways

  • Equity markets advanced in 4Q25, buoyed by moderating inflation and robust earnings. Technology remained dominant, fueled by accelerating artificial intelligence (AI) adoption, while industrials benefited from strong capital expenditure trends, although energy softened after early strength. Broader market participation persisted, with small caps and cyclicals contributing.
  • For the quarter ended December 31, 2025, the SMA outperformed the Index on both a gross- and net-of-fees basis due to favorable stock selection. Stock selection in information technology, communication services, and materials sectors contributed the most to performance. Conversely, selection within the health care, consumer discretionary, and energy sectors detracted from performance.
  • Looking ahead, investors face geopolitical risks and policy uncertainty. Market leadership is widening beyond mega-cap growth, supported by AI-driven innovation and sustained corporate capital expenditure. Opportunities are emerging in defensives and rate-sensitive sectors, reinforcing the need for nimble positioning amid evolving macro conditions.

Market review

U.S. equity markets closed 4Q25 on a positive note, driven by moderating inflation and robust earnings. The S&P 500 Index advanced 2.66%, and the technology-heavy Nasdaq Composite gained 2.57%. The healthcare and communications services sectors led, while real estate and utilities sectors lagged. Large cap stocks beat small cap stocks, and value outperformed growth stocks.

The U.S. Federal Reserve supported markets in 4Q25 with two 25-basis point rate cuts. At its December meeting, the Fed signaled a balanced tone and improved growth and inflation outlook. At the same time, artificial intelligence (AI) remained a dominant theme, with strong headlines around innovation and elevated capital investment by major technology firms. Increased spending on AI infrastructure and adoption trends reinforced optimism about its role as a key driver of future growth.

Portfolio review

For the quarter ended December 31, 2025, the SMA outperformed the Index on both a gross- and net-of-fees basis due to favorable stock selection. Stock selection in information technology, communication services, and materials sectors contributed the most to performance. Conversely, selection within the health care, consumer discretionary, and energy sectors detracted from performance.

At the individual stock level, owning non-benchmark in Alcoa Corp. (AA), overweight to Micron Technology, Inc. (MU), and not owning Meta Platforms Inc. Class A (META) were among the SMA's largest contributors for the quarter.

Owning non-benchmark position in Alcoa Corp. (AA) contributed to performance, driven by higher alumina and aluminum prices and supported by robust demand from automotive, aerospace, and renewable energy sectors.

Overweight to Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) contributed to performance, driven by strong 4Q25 earnings results. Both revenue and earnings per share (EPS) exceeded expectations, supported by robust 1Q26 guidance.

Not owning Meta Platforms Inc. Class A (META) contributed to performance. While 3Q25 results exceeded consensus, investor concerns over rising expenses and elevated capital expenditures weighed on the stock, offsetting strong fundamental factors.

At the individual stock level, not owning Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY), our position in Pinterest, Inc. Class A (PINS), and overweight to Williams-Sonoma, Inc. (WSM) were among the SMA's largest detractors for the quarter.

Not owning Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY) detracted from performance as the company delivered a strong quarter, driven by its incretin franchise. Full-year revenue and earnings guidance was raised above consensus, supported by robust demand for glucagon-like peptide (GLP-1) therapies such as Mounjaro and Zepbound.

Our position in Pinterest Inc, Class A (PINS) detracted from performance. While 3Q25 results were in line, user growth decelerated and weak 4Q25 guidance, driven by tariffs impacting home furnishings, pressured the stock.

Overweight to Williams-Sonoma, Inc. (WSM) detracted from Voya Balanced Large Cap Core-Value SMA (Merrill) performance as shares fell despite better-than-expected result. Weak forward guidance, tariff-related margin pressure, and softening e-commerce demand weighed on sentiment.

Outlook

The U.S. economy enters 2026 with a foundation of resilience. We expect moderate growth supported by consumer spending and productivity gains, helped by lower rates. The Fed's recent rate cut reflects heightened concern over labor market softening and confidence that tariff-driven inflation pressures will fade.

While the outlook suggests a soft landing with moderate growth and gradual disinflation, caution is warranted as geopolitical risks and policy uncertainty persist. In addition, broadening of the narrow market leadership beyond mega-cap growth, underpinned by AI innovation and corporate investment should support market growth.

data.title: S&P 500 Sees Small Decline In Week Without Major Market-Moving News
data.description:
After closing out the preceding week at a record high, the S&P 500 (SPX) clocked a new record high close of 6,977.27 on Monday, 12 January 2026, before retreating a half percent to end the week at 6,940.01, about 0.4% below its previous week's close.

There really wasn't any single catalyst to prompt the retreat. Instead, the general flow of news during the week was mildly negative for the outlook of large publicly traded firms, with the result being that stock prices ended the week marginally lower.

For example, inflation news during the week that was did little to influence expectations for how U.S. interest rates will change during 2026. The CME Group's FedWatch Tool continues to project the Fed will hold the Federal Funds Rate steady until 17 June (2026-Q2), when it anticipates a quarter-point rate cut. The tool forecasts another quarter-point reduction on 28 October (2026-Q4), six weeks later than it forecast a week earlier.

Perhaps the biggest surprise during the week was President Trump putting potential new tariffs on the table on Friday, 16 January 2026, for European nations opposing U.S. negotiations to acquire the territory of Greenland from Denmark. Which, considering how President Trump has transformed tariffs into a tool for the U.S.'s international diplomacy over the last year, isn't a terribly surprising development and did little to affect stock prices.

All in all, we find the trajectory of the S&P 500 is right about in the middle of the redzone forecast range shown on the latest update of the alternative futures chart for 2026-Q1.

Here are the market-moving headlines, such as they were, for the trading week ending on Friday, 16 January 2026.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Futures Slip As Trading Resumes
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Biggest stock movers Tuesday: NVDA, RAPT, and more. (00:15) NYSE develops platform for 24/7 trading of tokenized securities. (01:31) Tesla (TSLA) set to be early winner of Canada-China EV tariff deal - report. (02:34)

This is an abridged transcript.

Stock futures are down early on this Tuesday morning following President Trump's warning of mounting tariffs on key European partners blocking a U.S. bid for control of Greenland.

We’ll get to Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures in a few minutes. First, let’s take a look at a few of the biggest movers in premarket action.

Logitech (LOGI) -6%, NetApp (NTAP) -4%, and CDW (CDW) -2% - Shares declined after Morgan Stanley downgraded all three, warning of a “perfect storm” for IT hardware as corporate spending slows to its weakest pace in 15 years outside COVID-19.

A 4Q CIO survey pointed to softer demand, while resellers expect 30%–60% of customers to cut PC, server, and storage budgets amid component-driven price increases.

The firm said this marks only the “first cut” of a hardware down-cycle that could last three to five quarters.

CDW was cut to Equal-Weight (PT $141), Logitech to Underweight (PT $89), and NetApp to Underweight (PT $89).

The New York Stock Exchange, owned by Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), announced that it is developing a platform for 24/7 trading of tokenized U.S.-listed equities and ETFs, which are represented digitally using blockchain technology.

NYSE will seek regulatory approvals for the new platform, which will enable instant settlement, orders sized in dollar amounts, and stablecoin-based funding. It did not disclose when the platform will launch.

The platform's design combines NYSE's existing matching engine, which matches buy and sell orders, with blockchain-based post-trade systems, including the capability to support multiple chains for settlement and custody.

The platform is one component of ICE's (ICE) broader digital strategy, which includes preparing its clearing infrastructure to support 24/7 trading and the potential integration of tokenized collateral.

ICE (ICE) is working with banks to support tokenized deposits across its clearinghouses to help clearing members transfer and manage money outside traditional banking hours.

"Supporting tokenized securities is a pivotal step in ICE's strategy to operate on-chain market infrastructure for trading, settlement, custody, and capital formation in the new era of global finance," said Michael Blaugrund, vice president of strategic initiatives at ICE (ICE).

Tesla (TSLA) will likely be one of the first automakers to benefit from Canada's move to slash tariffs on Chinese-made electric vehicles, Reuters reported.

Canada will allow 49,000 EVs to be imported from China annually with a 6.1% tariff, down from the 100% rate that was in place since 2024, in exchange for tariff concessions on canola and other Canadian goods. The EV quota could rise to 70,000 within five years.

While Chinese EV makers don't have a significant sales presence in Canada, Tesla (TSLA) has an existing network of 39 stores in the country.

Tesla's (TSLA) biggest plant is in Shanghai, which is already equipped to build and export a Canada-specific version of its Model Y, according to the report.

It started shipping this version to Canada in 2023 but was forced to stop in 2024 after Ottawa imposed 100% tariffs on Chinese-made EVs. It currently ships Model Ys produced in Berlin to Canada.

To note, half of Canada's EV quota under the trade deal will be reserved for vehicles priced under C$35,000 ($25,192). Tesla (TSLA) model prices are above that number.

Low-cost cars from China's BYD (BYDDF) could benefit from that quota, but the company currently has negligible sales in Canada.

Other automakers that exported Chinese-made cars to Canada before the imposition of 100% tariffs include Volvo (VLVLY) and Polestar (PSNY), which are both owned by Chinese conglomerate Geely (GELYF).

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Fed's Powell to attend Supreme Court hearing on Lisa Cook case - report

Trump floats 200% tariffs on French wines over Macron's refusal to join 'Board of Peace'

OpenAI CFO says ARR hits above $20B in 2025

Catalyst watch:

  • Walmart (WMT) will be officially added to the Nasdaq 100 at the beginning of the trading day.

  • Aytu BioPharma (AYTU) will hold an Investor Day event in New York City.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is up 0.2% at $59/barrel. Bitcoin is down 1.5% at $91,000. Gold is up 1% at $4,726.

The FTSE 100 is down 1.2% and the DAX is down 1.3%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Rapt Therapeutics (RAPT) +64% - Shares jumped after GSK (GSK) agreed to acquire the biotech in a $2.2B deal, paying $58 per share in cash through a tender offer expected to launch within 10 business days.

data.title: Atlassian: AI Fears Are Overblown
data.description:
Since my last coverage, shares of Atlassian (TEAM) have plummeted by over 25% as a result of a broad selloff in software names, as fears of potential disruption related to AI have accelerated. Here, the driving factor was the announcement of Anthropic's Claude Cowork, a new autonomous AI Agent that can operate autonomously to manage workflows such as task management, document management, and file organization without requiring continuous human intervention. The news sparked renewed concerns that AI Agents could threaten the value proposition and business models of traditional companies selling SaaS products with a main revenue model built around seat license pricing and complex workflows. The narrative gained traction and momentum fast. If an AI Agent can do the work that many people are doing, do companies continue to have a need for so many licenses, tools, and layers of software products?

However, it's not just TEAM that has seen its stock crater. Other large-cap software names, such as Adobe (ADBE), Salesforce (CRM), and ServiceNow (NOW), were hit hard, although TEAM suffered by far the greatest losses as a result of a downrate from $240 to $210. The primary fear of AI agents today is that they are going to replace all of software. However, there are still differences between what the AI agents can do compared to software systems. Through its direct integration with a user's desktop, Claude Cowork can take on repetitive tasks and automate them, such as summarizing emails, document routing, or reorganizing files. Here, AI could improve the efficiency of existing software systems by automating repetitive administrative functions.

However, what AI Agents can't simply replicate is data ownership, stored inside the cloud. Here, Atlassian's cloud-first transition gives the company an enormous competitive advantage, in my opinion. As customers migrate their products from Jira, Confluence, and other related products to the cloud, the data will be embedded in Atlassian's cloud environment, and as such, will not easily migrate.

For the customer, it will be very costly, causing disruption and having a high operational risk if simply from Atlassian's cloud. As such, the decision to leave Atlassian is not just about the licenses but about how difficult and expensive it would be to move. A company is faced with the challenges of migrating large amounts of both structured (databases/systems with set data) and unstructured (files, videos, etc.) data. They would also have to rebuild their custom workflow and automations and retrain employees.

Currently, a large company with, let's say, 5000-10000 employees would likely spend anywhere around $1.5–3.5 million annually on Atlassian, before discounts or enterprise pricing adjustments. Currently, switching over completely to Claude Cowork would be estimated to cost $12-24 million annually, mostly because Cowork pricing is per individual user and not volume-discounted in the same way yet. Either way, I doubt that most large customers would find the effort of switching to a different provider worth the cost savings, especially considering that other tools won't be for free either.

Arguably, there is a higher risk for smaller customers to create their own in-house project management tools through AI. However, these come with significant risks, especially related to cybersecurity, software bugs, and scalability. Storing sensitive data insecurely or software bugs could cause operational disruptions and significant reputational damage, which many smaller companies and startups are not willing to take. It's also not financially lucrative. A startup with 50 employees would likely spend $6k-9k annually on Atlassian. Even by using AI, an in-house engineered project management tool would cost anywhere from $100k to $160k per year, and this does not account for the effort and the potential disruptions as a result of bugs and security leaks. So, AI won't simply code up a whole new project management tool for a fraction of the price for every company, as the market's reaction suggests. After all, Atlassian is also integrating its own AI tools:

Our relentless pace of AI innovation is driving results as we grew cloud revenue in Q1 to $998 million, up 26% year-over-year, and surpassed 3.5 million monthly active users of our AI capabilities, up 50% quarter-over-quarter - Atlassian's CEO and co-Founder Mike Cannon-Brookes.

Thus, in my opinion, AI has the potential to actually increase the total installed base value of Atlassian. The productive execution of work using AI technologies (via Jira, automated documentation through Confluence, smarter prioritization, dependency mapping, and cross-team visibility) allows for significantly enhanced productivity while maintaining established governance structures. As a result, Atlassian becomes the governance/control mechanism over any AI-generated productivity work instead of being negatively impacted by AI and/or automation loss of revenue.

That said, based upon current analyst consensus, Atlassian's P/E ratio would shrink to just 11.2 by 2030 and below 5x by 2035, given its current share price. Given that the stock now also trades at a reasonable P/E ratio, this provides an extra layer of security, even if revenue growth were to slow down. The company also approved a new share repurchase program for up to $2.5 billion of Class A common stock, to commence following the completion of the $1.5 billion program previously authorized in September 2024. At the current market cap of just above $30 billion, this represents 8% of the company's entire market cap. Given the historically low valuation, I could imagine that the company approves further buybacks in the near future.

Takeaways

I think the market is currently overreacting to fears that AI will make software obsolete. Switching costs for large customers are very real, and building own-cloud native tools still requires significant effort with risks for operational disruptions. Atlassian continues to execute against their cloud strategy to deepen the customer footprint and embed AI directly into its solutions.

Although there is a good chance to see a further pullback from here in the short term and medium term, given the uncertainty of the overall market, cautious enterprise spending, and negative sentiment regarding the development of AI solutions, the long-term growth story remains healthy, in my opinion. If Atlassian's next quarter indicates that AI fears are overblown, in the form of sustainable demand, stable customer retention, and increased cloud momentum, TEAM shares could rebound quickly.

We have seen examples of this type of pricing action occur within other companies, such as Meta Platforms (META), Netflix (NFLX), and Alphabet (GOOGL), which were seemingly written off by the market, only to surge to new all-time highs after sentiment improved. Atlassian could be at a similar inflection point, as the market has become extremely fearful of software stocks. Thus, in my opinion, Atlassian currently trades at a heavy discount and could rebound swiftly in the future.

data.title: FETH: Not A 'Buy' Without Staking (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
It has been about 6 months since I last covered the Fidelity Ethereum Fund ETF (FETH) for Seeking Alpha. To briefly recap my thoughts on the fund, it was my preferred spot Ethereum USD (ETH-USD) ETF back when it launched in July 2024. I have generally liked that Fidelity relies on self-custody for asset holdings rather than third-party providers like Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN), though Fidelity has deviated from the self-custody strategy in the newer Fidelity Solana Fund ETF (FSOL). In this update, we'll look at Ethereum's key metrics for Q4-25 as well as capital flow considerations broadly and for FETH specifically.

Ethereum Network Metrics

Q4-25 was a continuation of the broad trend that we've seen for Ethereum over the last several quarters. Namely, on-chain activity is up when viewed through usage metrics like Daily Active Addresses and transactions; each of which was up by nearly 30% year-over-year. However, we continue to see what I view to be one of the most important economic numbers for the network declining.

Ethereum Q4-25 Q3-25 Q4-25 YoY QoQ
DAAs (000s) 400.8 516.0 509.9 27.2% -1.2%
Transactions (millions) 112.1 147.3 145.2 29.5% -1.4%
Fees (millions) $552.10 $125.40 $76.60 -86.1% -38.9%

Source: Artemis Analytics.

Fees in the quarter were down 86% year-over-year from $552 million in Q4-24 to just $76.6 million in Q4-25. The sequential decline in fees was also high at nearly 40% in a single quarter. This decline in fees took Ethereum from the top network in the public blockchain industry in Q4-24 down to the third-ranked chain by fees in Q4-25.

Even though network fees have essentially collapsed over the last several years, Ethereum's stablecoin footprint continues to be terrific. Not only has the chain seen more than 89% year-over-year growth in stablecoin supply from Q4-24 to Q4-25, but the actual usage of those coins has seen tremendous growth.

Stable Tx Vol Q4-25 Q3-25 Q4-25 YoY QoQ
Total Market (trillions) $10.2 $15.8 $19.7 93.1% 24.7%
Ethereum (trillions) $2.3 $5.0 $7.5 226.1% 50.0%
ETH Share 18.40% 24.04% 27.57% 49.9% 14.7%
Supply (billions) $87,100 $130,300 $164,700 89.1% 26.4%

Source: Artemis Analytics.

In the last three months, Ethereum settled $7.5 billion in stablecoin transaction volume. This was good for 226% year-over-year growth and 27.6% shares of the total stablecoin market. Importantly, Ethereum's share of stablecoin transaction volume also grew both year-over-year and sequentially.

Digital Asset Capital Flows

Capital markets are still viewing Ethereum favorably from where I sit. During the full year 2025, Ethereum saw $12.7 billion in positive net flows into investment products. This was good for just under 27% of the total asset flows in the market. That trend is generally continuing in 2026, with $553 million in year-to-date net flows giving Ethereum 23% share of capital flows.

Asset (millions) YTD Flows AUM 2025 Flows
Bitcoin $1,664.0 $149,776 $26,984
Ethereum $552.9 $27,543 $12,698
Multi-asset -$32.1 $6,881 -$214.0
XRP $108.1 $3,865 $3,697.0
Solana $75.8 $3,832 $3,562.0
ETH Dominance 23.0% 14.2% 26.9%

Source: CoinShares, Bloomberg as of 1/16/25.

Considering Ethereum's investment AUM in the digital asset space is just 14.2% share of the market, Ethereum should continue to see a bid from investors if the network can continue to scale both stablecoin supply and stablecoin usage on-chain.

FETH Vs. Peers

While the market demand for Ethereum products in 2026 remains strong relative to AUM share, not all the digital asset ETF providers are seeing the same success. Despite having the third largest AUM in the broad space, Fidelity's products are underperforming a broad market that has already seen $2.4 billion in net inflows to begin 2026:

Provider (millions) MTD Flows YTD Flows AUM

2025 Flows
iShares $1,798 $1,798 $87,477 $35,056
Grayscale $28 $28 $27,636 -$2,851
Fidelity -$126 -$126 $23,048 $2,151
Bitwise $262 $262 $8,448 $1,105
Volatility Shares -$77 -$77 $4,352 $372
Total $2,404 $2,404 $193,556 $47,153

Source: CoinShares, Bloomberg as of 1/16/25.

With $126 million already coming out of Fidelity products to begin the year, this could actually get worse for the provider before it gets better, given the fact that it has been Fidelity's Bitcoin USD (BTC-USD) product rather than its Ethereum product that has created the outflow year to date. Fidelity's ETH product has generated a little under $23 million in positive inflows. But that inflow is lagging competing funds:

Fund Expense Ratio Staking? YTD Net Flows
iShares Ethereum Trust ETF (ETHA) 0.25% Pending $369.90
Grayscale Ethereum Mini Staking ETF (ETH) 0.15% Yes $137.30
Bitwise Ethereum ETF (ETHW) 0.20% Pending $59.90
FETH 0.25% Pending $22.60
21Shares Ethereum ETF (TETH) 0.21% Pending $6.60

Source: Farside.

The biggest issue I see for FETH at this time is that the fund lacks staking. While it is widely expected that the SEC will eventually approve staking for FETH, among other funds, right now, Grayscale is the only provider that does indeed have staking enabled for the fund assets. In a prior article, I detailed why I liked Grayscale's Mini ETF compared to Grayscale's original Ethereum product despite the lower staking ratio. Until FETH has staking enabled, the Grayscale Ethereum Mini ETF is likely the better long-term bet due to its lower fee and staking of assets.

Risks

There are numerous risks to consider before allocating capital to digital assets. Those risks include, but are not limited to, declining network usage, potential for speculative capital outflows, sales from DAT companies, and regulatory headwinds. Aside from all the standard risks that go with buying digital assets and their proxies, these things generally trade at nosebleed valuations when compared to traditional equities.

For instance, on a circulating price-to-fees multiple, ETH has never in its history been as 'overvalued' as it is today. At 3,260x fees, the decline in network fees coupled with the elevated price of the token has created a parabolic rise in the P/F multiple. I'm very concerned about this personally and see it as possible, if not likely, that Ethereum's potential success in stablecoin payments is already being priced in by the market. Not only is stablecoin payment success far from a sure thing broadly within the industry, but there is no guarantee that Ethereum will remain the long-term winner should such success manifest.

Closing Summary

I'm no longer personally holding FETH. My personal Ethereum exposure is limited to on-chain holdings and a speculative position through Grayscale's Mini fund. In the event FETH does get staking enabled, I could see myself revisiting the product. But for now, I'm downgrading FETH on two core factors: I like an alternative product better, and I'm highly concerned about the token's valuation.

data.title: Stock Picker's Guide To 2026: How AI And Earnings Will Shape Stock Market Trends
data.description:
Transcript

Oscar Pulido: After three straight years of robust returns, investors are asking, can stocks maintain their momentum in 2026? This year's backdrop looks very different from the one that carried markets higher in 2025. AI continues to reshape corporate strategy, but the story is no longer just about technology leadership. It's about the scale of capital being deployed, the pressure it places on balance sheets, and the way this spending is beginning to shape the broader economy.

Welcome to The Bid, where we break down what's happening in the markets and explore the forces changing the economy and finance. I'm Oscar Pulido.

Today, Carrie King, Global Chief Investment Officer of BlackRock's Fundamental Equities group, joins us to explore the opportunities and risks that may lie ahead. We'll talk about whether AI can live up to the expectations baked into today's valuations. How companies are navigating a period of high investment and evolving macro conditions, and where Carrie sees opportunities emerging, both within and beyond AI.

Carrie, thank you so much for joining us on The Bid.

Carrie King: Thank you for having me.

Oscar Pulido: So, Carrie, US stocks finished the year with double-digit gains, once again. It didn't necessarily feel like we'd get to that, given all the concerns around tariffs and the occasional fatigue around AI and the pullbacks that we saw in some of the AI-related names. So as we look ahead to this year, what do you foresee for the stock market in 2026?

Carrie King: Oscar, I'm sanguine about the stock market in 2026, and let me explain to you why. Let's decompose the drivers of stock market returns. One is what will the earnings growth rate be? And two is what kind of multiple will investors be willing to put on that?

The growth rate of earnings will be based on fundamentals, which I think have a very strong outlook next year. We've got AI spending; we've got fed cuts. And I think the multiple is causing a lot of consternation for some market observers. It's a seemingly high 22 times earnings today, which reminds people it's the same level it was at the peak of the .com bubble. And that didn't end well! But let me dispel why we should have any concern about that.

Number one is that high P/E multiples in and of themselves do not derail bull markets. And number two is, if you look at the quality of companies today, they're deserving of a higher multiple. The quality of companies has never been higher than it is today. And if we compare companies today -- compared to the .com bubble era, we think there's an adjustment to the P/E multiple to account for that higher quality. So, we think the P/E multiple is really something closer to 17 times earnings, which is a long-term average.

So, 17 multiple on earnings, which is a long-term average on earnings per share growth, which should sustain in the mid-teens. Which is an above average growth rate. I think that's a very strong setup for positive returns in 2026.

Oscar Pulido: You talked about AI, and we have to talk about it. It's a theme that has become very persistent. Do you still think that the AI theme is going to be what dominates the markets in 2026, as was the case in the last couple of years?

Carrie King: So Oscar, let's level set on exactly what we mean when we're talking about AI, will it dominate? Let's go back to 2022 in November when ChatGPT first burst onto the scene seemingly out of nowhere. The first order of business was to invest in what was most obvious, which was the benefit accruing to, let's call it, the picks and shovels. So the semiconductor, the semi cap equipment companies. And let's talk about Nvidia (NVDA) as an example. In November of 2022, Nvidia was $12 per share. Today it's about $180. So a 15-fold increase in a few short years. NVIDIA's revenues coming into 2023, or the AI era, were $27 billion. Next year they're set to be $320 billion, so a 12-fold increase. Consider that during that period we have seen a 1500 basis point expansion in the gross margin for the company, amazing! Now, do we think we will see that magnitude of improvement and change in the future as we've seen in the past? I don't think that's on deck for us.

Let's look at the second order of investment, in AI last year, 2025, we saw that enthusiasm spread beyond the picks and shovels to the data centers and the data center build-out, which drove shares of utilities, power companies, industrials, companies involved in lighting, air conditioning components. So, 2026, will AI dominate the conversation? Yes, I think AI will dominate the conversation, but will the AI beneficiaries be a different bucket of companies? And I think that is very likely, or there will be an additional bucket of companies.

So I think we may go from focusing on the CapEx, the spending, the revenue to the cost savings, the efficiency gains, and the operational optimizations. So yes, AI will continue to dominate, but as it evolves, the beneficiary may be a different group of companies.

Oscar Pulido: AI, as a mega force, starts to touch a lot of different companies in a lot of different ways, and so a lot of different ways to benefit from that theme. That theme, as you know, is also very prominent in the BlackRock Investment Institute's 2026 outlook. When we spoke to Jean Boivin, he talked about AI as the micro becoming the macro, meaning that the capital expenditures that we're seeing towards the buildout of artificial intelligence now has a direct impact on economic growth, and it's been one of the things keeping the economy afloat. There's some concern that if those investments don't go as planned, they could not only just be bad for the market, they could be bad for the economy. How should investors be thinking about this unprecedented spending?

Carrie King: So, AI is absolutely a generational technology with lasting impact, I think, on the economy and businesses around the globe, particularly in the US. What I think is inevitable that everyone needs to be aware of and mindful is that I believe there will be a timing mismatch between the spending that is taking place and that will take place and the return on that investment. What will the duration, or timing of that mismatch be? It's really unclear. Therefore, I think it's imperative that all investors, particularly those looking to benefit from AI, prepare for bouts of volatility and stay very firm in a long-term view. And we're seeing already a lot of benefit.

IBM did a survey in May of last year, and what they heard from business leaders and CEOs is that about half of CEOs in varying industries are seeing value from AI beyond just cost reductions. They also noted that 85% of CEOs in this survey expect a positive return on investment in AI by 2027.

And with a little help from AI and fundamental equities, our research shows that twice as many companies in the third quarter noted AI savings and productivity enhancements than we saw in the prior year. So, for example, JPMorgan noted $2 billion of expense equaled about $2 billion of benefit. And Citigroup noted that AI was freeing up about a hundred thousand development hours per week. So pretty powerful results coming from AI investment. And then in terms of the build-out of AI, what we are starting to see, and there's a lot of discussion about, is companies are starting to use leverage to fund the build-out, and that's raising eyebrows a little bit, I would say. So, we would note that it's the large-cap companies with ample free cash flows, I think, are the safest way to play this trend.

Oscar Pulido: And one of the trends that I think you're alluding to is AI as a productivity enhancer and in that investment outlook that BlackRock Investment Institute published, Jean talked about a growth trend in the US that has been consistent for 150 years, which is a 2% economic growth rate. And what he talks about, and I think what you're alluding to, is the opportunity to maybe break out of that growth trend, and AI could be part of that.

We've been talking about AI, we've been talking about the US, so maybe we can shift gears a little bit because there were some international markets that outperformed the US in 2025. Can you tell us about those and where do you see the opportunities outside the US in 2026?

Carrie King: Sure and absolutely, they definitely were the unsung heroes of 2025. To your point. We see three key areas that have done well, and we think are poised to continue to show strength. One of them is in emerging markets. We saw 125 global central banks cut rates in 2025 and that includes some very large emerging market economies like Mexico and Turkey, and Brazil is now seeing lower inflation, which kind of opens the door for Brazil to follow suit.

And of course, US lowering rates, which we just saw, is good for EM. So those are some cyclical tailwinds that we think can keep this trend in place. In addition, there's global friction and redrawing of supply chains continues. And this is creating opportunities for some countries in particular.

So, Korea, for example, is really becoming a powerhouse in battery and memory supply chain. They're owning the space. And Dubai and the UAE is expanding as a global financial center that will bring along loads of investment into the area. So, a lot of very exciting things happening.

Beyond EM, we still like Japan. It's been a trend that's been in place for a bit now, but we see a continuation. We see in Japan decades of deflation turning to inflation. And consider this, over 50% of personal household assets are held in cash in Japan. So we think that going from deflation to inflation will spur consumer spending, which will benefit the economy and risk assets. Beyond just that, there is tremendous institutional reform pressure in Japan, and where we're seeing that is in corporate governance. We're seeing it in transparency and valuations of companies. We're seeing companies streamline operations, and this is all accruing to shareholders through better pricing of their stocks, better valuations, and more cash and dividends being returned to shareholders of Japanese shares.

Oscar Pulido: I'm wondering if we can come back to earnings, because I have to imagine that when you have rates being cut across the globe, that has to be a good catalyst for earnings. You mentioned a very strong earnings picture in 2025. Talk a little bit more about where you see earnings going this year.

Carrie King: I think we can sustain mid-teens growth again in 2026. But I do think it will be different going forward. I think we will see a different composition of earnings growth across the S&P 500 as a proxy. And why do I say that? There should be a widening gap between the group of seven companies, the largest companies, in the US that basically dominated all of the earnings per share growth in the US. The gap should widen between them and the other 493 companies. So, these seven companies, which you've probably heard referred to as the magnificent seven, we think the growth rate will decelerate to about 20% next year. Still extremely strong and healthy. But the peak was 37% in 2024, so a deceleration.

At the same time, we think the other 493 will see an acceleration in earnings per share growth to the double-digit level from either declining or up low single digits in the past couple of years. And among those other 493, I think the strongest growth we will see coming from technology companies, industrial companies and materials companies. As you point out, cyclical tailwind from rate cuts, industrial companies are seeing strong order book growth.

A few of the things I mentioned already, which include reshoring and the consumer. We hear a lot about the K-shaped consumer economy. The high end is doing well, the low end is doing poor, but what we are seeing is, and there's very good visibility here, as we are seeing strong demand for airlines travel, hotels. And if you think about those sectors, just think about yourself personally. Great visibility. I know myself, I have travel booked into the next 12 months. So, there's a lot of visibility on strong demand for travel, and then other pockets of strength should include financials where we should see the benefit of capital markets strength, M&A opening up, less regulation under the current administration. And we're seeing a pickup in lending as well, which continue again with cyclical tailwinds.

And then I like to just mention, like, one sleeper - healthcare. I've mentioned in this forum a couple of times, we'll call it that I was early, but it's starting to show signs of life. It's been a laggard. But I would highlight the shares are very discounted relative to their history. We have seen, and in this category, 80% of companies have guided upward in their guidance, which is more than any other sector. We've got innovation in this sector. We've got strong balance sheets. We have a demographic tailwind, and we're starting to see policy concerns start to wind down with the companies negotiating some pricing control concerns. So, the setup is pretty strong for healthcare as well.

Oscar Pulido: Maybe we can pick up there because it makes me think back to this concept of diversification is hard to find. Again, the BlackRock Investment Institute in its Outlook talks about this diversification mirage, that so many things are tied to the AI theme and the AI buildout that where do you go to find diversification? What is that plan B that investors should have as they go into the year?

Carrie King: I love that question. It's fantastic. Everybody should be spending more time thinking about it. And I'll share with you something that I find very interesting. If you think you want to diversify away from growth, it almost feels like why would you ever do that? But there will be a time you need to do it. Value indexes are more growthy today than they have been in a decade. So, if you think you're going to buy a passive value index to diversify yourself away from growth, you need to think again and maybe think about an active value strategy that has value exposure against a value benchmark, if you will. That was a lot of words, but, boiling it down to a point, it's just that a passive index in value may not be giving you the exposure you're expecting.

Oscar Pulido: And value, again, being companies that are typically trading at PE multiples below the market average. Maybe these haven't been some of the faster-growing companies, but they have a bit of a defensive quality if we get into some periods of volatility. I think that's the right way to think about value.

Carrie King: Absolutely.

Oscar Pulido: Carrie, it is the New Year; we usually have New Year's resolutions that come about. I'd be curious if you have any personal New Year's resolutions, but first let's talk about what resolution you think our listeners could have when it comes to their equity portfolios and just their investment strategy for the year ahead?

Carrie King: I will share something that I'm always challenging myself for my two decades of investing. Embrace volatility. Learn to be comfortable with volatility and continue to take a long-term view. I think that's always important. But in the context of this AI conversation, I think it's even more important in today's environment. It's inevitable that volatility will come, but it provides the most fruitful opportunities for investors that are able to take a long-term view. So be comfortable with the uncomfortable.

And on a personal note, I'm going to enter my next decade of trying to give up chocolate once again!

Oscar Pulido: Well, Carrie, we wish you much success with that personal resolution. I myself am going to go back to the tried and tested, make sure I'm getting exercise in my daily regime that's good for the mind and good for the body as well. But you've given us some great things to think about with respect to the equity markets. And the thing I keep thinking about is that it looks like the outlook is a very good one, one that sees performance coming from a lot of different sectors. One where AI is really impacting a number of areas positively, but the point about embrace volatility - things don't go straight up - so when those time periods exist, lean into that. And that's a good time to remind yourself to be a long-term investor.

Carrie, these are great words. We look forward to reviewing them over the course of the year and see how they play out. Thank you for bringing these insights to The Bid.

Carrie King: Thank you very much.

Oscar Pulido: Thanks for listening to this episode of The Bid.

Disclosure

data.title: Franco-Nevada: A High-Quality Compounder Worth Paying Up For (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since I last covered Franco-Nevada Corporation (FNV) with a Hold rating, shares are up 27.65% on a total return basis versus 2.99% for the S&P 500. Over the same period, gold rose 11.4%, while silver soared an eye-catching 84.2%.

Franco-Nevada has long been one of my favourite businesses—and it has outperformed the Nasdaq and S&P 500 on a total return basis since its inception in 2007. In this update, I will explain why that longstanding quality story has now evolved into a clearer growth story—and why recent execution, improving optionality, and accelerating cash flows have pushed me to upgrade my view from Hold to Buy. I will walk through the latest earnings, the medium-term growth outlook, and potential upside catalysts like Cobre Panama, and why valuation still looks reasonable for long-term investors despite the strong run in the share price.

Q3 Earnings

Franco-Nevada delivered Q3 earnings on the 4th of November, and for the third consecutive time in 2025, set record quarterly results.

Total revenue skyrocketed 77% year-over-year to $487.7 million, while adjusted EBITDA rose 81% to $427.3 million. Perhaps most impressive was the margin expansion: as gold prices climbed, FNV's cash cost per gold equivalent ounce remained relatively stable, resulting in a staggering margin of $3,116 per gold equivalent ounce!

At the end of Q2, revenue from precious metals accounted for 81% of total revenue, but that has now lifted to 85% by the end of Q3. Franco-Nevada's quality is reinforced by the fact that its portfolio consists of 434 assets across different geographies—120 producing, 38 in an advanced stage, and 276 exploration assets—providing cash flow visibility now and a clear runway for future growth.

Growth Outlook

FNV looks set for continued growth, targeting roughly a 50% rise in Gold Equivalent Ounces by 2029 versus 2024 levels. That kind of growth is being driven by a string of six acquisitions in quick succession.

The real swing factor, though, could be Cobre Panama—as Panama's president recently hinted at a possible resolution to the mine's closure (with a decision likely to be reached by June). The Panama government allowed the sale of 11,208 GEOs from copper concentrate in Q3 that had been stored on site while the mine was not in production. Although this should not be read as a strong signal that the mine will restart, it does signal that channels of communication remain open and that the government is willing to take pragmatic, incremental steps to preserve the asset's economic value—my base case for 2026 is that several long-stalled, politically sensitive projects begin to make "meaningful progress" toward restart, as higher precious and industrial metal prices make long-running disputes feel far more negotiable once forgone tax and royalty revenue become harder to ignore. If Cobre Panama restarts, combined with production ramp-ups at Tocantinzinho and Greenstone, as well as recovery to nameplate at Salares Norte, I think FNV could realistically hit its ambitious five-year GEO target.

What also stood out from the results was management highlighting a strategic evolution in how they acquire assets. CEO Paul Brink mentioned FNV's role as a "financial backer" for best-in-class teams, singling out their equity and royalty partnerships with G Mining Ventures (Tocantinzinho) and Discovery Silver (Porcupine). Just take a look at Discovery Silver Corp. (DSVSF)—admittedly one that had flown under my radar as it is not one of the stocks that I currently cover—but it is up an incredible 951% over the past year.

I think that FNV's approach of backing strong operators, combined with its underutilized Australian royalty land, could lead to something interesting in the years ahead. On this subject, management remarked in Q3 Earnings that:

We have added a new person to our team in Australia, Matt Selby, out of Perth, who's driving our business development there. We would like to grow our business in Australia. As you know, we have a ton of royalties that cover a huge amount of land in Australia, but it's not yet a big part of our revenue. I think there are particularly good prospects, and we're very keen to find good teams in Australia that we can back and potentially do something similar to what we've done with g-mining or discovery in the country.

FNV ended the quarter debt-free with $1.85 billion in available capital. On top of that, it generates roughly $350-400 million in cash each quarter. Management has earned this luxury of being able to cherry-pick best-in-class projects and to back operators they believe in, armed with the firepower to make meaningful bets without risking the balance sheet. I think this puts them in an enviable position and bodes well for shareholders.

Analyst Recommendations and Earnings Estimates

Of the four analysts who have published recommendations on the Bloomberg Terminal in 2026 to date, all carry a Buy/Outperform rating, with a consensus 12-month average price target of $284.18—implying 16.59% upside to the current share price.

FNV has now delivered four consecutive quarterly earnings beats, with each of the last three quarters setting new records. I think Q4 earnings are shaping up to be blockbuster as well, as gold and silver soared to new nose-bleed heights. Analyst expectations reflect this momentum, with consensus estimates effectively extrapolating the run of record earnings into at least the first half of 2026. That optimism is not without precedent either—this is a business that has increased its annual dividend for 18 consecutive years, funded through royalty and streaming cash flows that require minimal sustaining capital and carry no operating cost inflation.

Valuation

I prefer using FWD Price/Cash Flows when valuing royalty and streaming companies, as this tells me what I am paying for the underlying operations relative to the cash flows they are forecast to generate.

I continue to view FNV as the highest quality player in the sector—and it logically follows that FNV would have the highest 5-year average FWD P/OCF ratio amongst peers. However, what stands out now is that the current FWD P/CF ratio for FNV sits below both that of WPM and OR. Also, after the recent strong run in precious metal prices, FNV's peers all have ratios that are 40% or more above their own historic 5-year average, while that number is just 18% for FNV.

The Technical Take

FNV is comfortably trading above its 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving averages, having decisively broken out to fresh highs. Technicals remain constructive, with the MACD and RSI firmly in bullish territory, though the latter hints at shares being overbought at these levels. The technically important 61.8% Fibonacci retracement level sits at $222—roughly 9% below the current share price. While a pullback to this zone or the 50-day moving average at $208 would offer a more conservative entry point, the trend remains firmly intact as long as shares hold above it, suggesting momentum could carry them higher from here.

Takeaway

I am upgrading FNV from Hold to Buy. The reality is that there is simply too much growth and optionality ahead to ignore. While shares may not be at the most attractive entry point today—and a welcome pullback could offer a slightly cheaper opportunity—I still think shares offer value here. FNV is one of those rare high-quality businesses that you just hold and let it compound over time. I am comfortable with a $297 price target, implying 22% upside from current levels. That target reflects the average of the three most recent institutional analyst forecasts on the Bloomberg Terminal—and still strikes me as slightly conservative in light of FNV's accelerating cash flows and expanding growth pipeline.

data.title: Capstone Copper: Some Near-Term Turbulence, But Good Long-Term Value
data.description:
Overview

Capstone Copper (CS:CA) is a copper mining company listed in both Canada and Australia. It produces almost exclusively copper, unlike many other mining companies that often mine more of a mix of metals.

The company has 4 producing mines in Chile, Mexico, and the U.S. There is also the Santo Domingo development project, where the company recently divested 25% to Orion Resource Partners LP, not to overextend its balance sheet, taking this project to production.

Production

Capstone did, during the last week, release its Q4 2025 and full-year production numbers. Q4 was another record quarter, and the company came in at the lower end of the annual production guidance range.

Cozamin has performed in line with guidance during 2025, while Mantoverde and Pinto Valley have underperformed slightly. Mantoverde experienced some unplanned downtime in the September-November period due to a ball mill motor failure and maintenance following that. With that said, we can see in the chart below that the throughput in December was above design levels, which is an encouraging sign for the future.

Mantos Blancos had a good year, with production above the annual guidance range due to strong throughput in a couple of quarters and higher grades.

Growth

The company has yet to provide official guidance for the year, but production is likely to be softer in the beginning of 2026 by the looks of things. Capstone is currently in a labor dispute with one of the unions at Mantoverde, which has gone on strike.

Capstone did last year "successfully negotiate new three-year collective bargaining agreements with the three other unions at Mantoverde," and the higher copper price has likely provided this union with more leverage in the negotiations. Production at Mantoverde is expected to be running at 50-75% capacity during this strike.

This will have a material impact on the consolidated financials in the near term, given that Mantoverde has accounted for roughly 50% of adjusted EBITDA lately.

The near-term growth for Capstone is from the Mantoverde Optimized project ("MV-O"), which can take production up towards 280 kt per year. MV-O has a very manageable capital cost of $176M. This can be financed from the existing liquidity position and ongoing cash flow, especially if the strong copper price holds going forward. The project is currently under construction, with ramp-up scheduled for late 2026 or early 2027.

The main source of growth for Capstone is otherwise the Santo Domingo project, which can take production up towards 400 kt per year, and it is expected to be sanctioned in the second half of 2026. This is a more sizable project, though, with an initial capital cost estimated to be $2.3B.

There are plenty of other sources of growth that the company is looking at with the existing producing assets, even if I suspect Capstone will be busy with MV-O and Santo Domingo over the next couple of years. I do think the list below highlights the potential in Capstone.

Valuation & Conclusion

Using the latest share price of Capstone and the financials as of Q3 2025, the company has a market cap of $8.0B and an enterprise value of $9.0B. The estimated EBITDA is between $1.5B and $2.0B over the next few years for Capstone, which gives us a forward-looking EV to EBITDA of 4.5-6.0.

This is, in my view, an attractive value for this quality producer with all its production in the Americas. The estimates have increased over the last 6 months as the copper price has rallied, but I highly doubt the latest estimates reflect the latest copper price. So, the EBITDA estimates will likely continue to increase if copper prices remain firm.

The stock price performance of Capstone has done well over the last year, up 72%, due to the strong copper price. However, we can see from the chart below that Capstone has underperformed the Global X Copper ETF (COPX) over this period.

The downtime at Mantoverde during September-November and now a strike at its flagship mine are likely the main reasons for the recent underperformance. I would suggest zooming out here and focusing on the solid historical operating performance, the good jurisdictions, and growth projects. So, I rate Capstone as a buy, and even if the stock hasn't corrected much, I view the relative underperformance as a decent buying opportunity.

All mining companies carry risks as well. There are jurisdictional risks, even if Capstone has better jurisdictions than many other copper mining companies in my view. There are execution risks taking both MV-O and Santo Domingo to production, even if MV-O is a much smaller project where I am not overly concerned.

Capstone had $623M in attributable net debt at the end of Q3 2025, and the financial leverage will increase once Santo Domingo is commissioned, but the choice to divest 25% for up to $360M means the balance sheet will likely not get overextended. The very strong copper prices we have seen lately will no doubt boost earnings and cash flow, but they will also increase the liquidity position materially, mitigating any potential balance sheet risks the market might have for this growth company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ardelyx: IBSRELA Momentum Drives Opportunity
data.description:
Ardelyx (ARDX) has developed into a commercial execution machine with two FDA-approved, first-in-class medications. Coming into 2026, Ardelyx has a dual-franchise model, which will allow for strong revenue growth while providing a diversified revenue floor. Historically, the nephrology space has been a difficult area for investors due to the complexity of reimbursement; however, I believe the rapid increase in IBSRELA uptake indicates that Ardelyx has identified a significant gap in the irritable bowel syndrome with constipation market.

Latest Financials

Total product revenues were in excess of $378 million for the fiscal year 2025 and increased 18% versus 2024. However, the nature of the growth in the top line is significant, as the revenue generated in 2025 was primarily the result of the performance of the company's product IBSRELA, which generated in excess of $274 million for the entire year of 2025. This represents an increase of greater than 70% when compared to the same period in 2024. When a commercialized drug product reaches this level of penetration in the market within a few years of launching, the product has achieved success based upon a combination of increasing prescription rates, improved patient compliance, and repeat use.

In addition to the success of IBSRELA, the company's other product, XPHOZAH, which is used to treat hyperphosphatemia, also generated in excess of $100 million in revenue for the fiscal year 2025. Although the growth trend of XPHOZAH may not be as aggressive as that of IBSRELA, the achievement of over $100 million in revenue for a drug product that is treating a condition that is currently being treated with generic binders is a confirmation of the medical need for a novel mechanism of action. The breakdown of the performance of both products in the fourth quarter of 2025 provides additional insight into the dynamic of the company's products, with IBSRELA generating approximately $87 million and XPHOZAH generating approximately $28 million. The fact that the performance of both products in the fourth quarter of 2025 is consistent with their previous quarters indicates that the company has established stable commercialization dynamics and is experiencing predictable increases in revenue.

One of the key aspects of the company's recent financial report is its balance sheet. As most recently reported, Ardelyx had approximately $265 million in cash, cash equivalents, and investments. In guidance for 2026, Ardelyx indicated that it expects to generate between $110 and $120 million in revenue from XPHOZAH and between $410 and $430 million in revenue from IBSRELA. Therefore, the company is projecting that its two products will generate in excess of $520 million in combined product revenue in 2026 and has ample means to fund future development and expansion.

Pipeline Expansion and Developments

While the main driver for short-term value creation at Ardelyx will be the commercialization of IBSRELA, the company has simultaneously been progressing a product pipeline that utilizes the company's internal competency to inhibit NHE3 (sodium-hydrogen exchanger type 3) activity.

The major development here is that the company initiated a Phase 3 clinical trial to evaluate the safety and efficacy of IBSRELA in patients suffering from chronic idiopathic constipation. This is a strategic expansion of the highest magnitude. The mechanism of action of tenapanor is not solely limited to treating irritable bowel syndrome; its capacity to inhibit sodium absorption and enhance intestinal fluid is theoretically relevant to the larger constipation treatment space. By initiating this Phase 3 clinical trial, which is expected to be completed by the end of 2027, the company is also expanding the total addressable market for its lead product.

In addition to utilizing tenapanor to treat patients who suffer from CIC, the company is developing RDX10531, which aims to be the next generation of an NHE3 inhibitor. By developing RDX10531, the company is essentially creating its own competition by ensuring that it owns the intellectual property rights related to NHE3 inhibitors should there be a shift in standards of care relative to the use of such inhibitors in the future. While this asset is still in the early stages of its development, it provides the company with optionality and potential partnerships down the road.

Strategic Impact of Patent Extension

I believe that an underappreciated feature of the company’s latest update is the Notice of Allowance for the new patent covering the commercial formulations of both IBSRELA and XPHOZAH. The new patent expires on December 6, 2041. In standard biopharma valuation models, analysts are usually applying rapid decay to revenue after the expiration of the initial composition of matter patents.

The previous patents for IBSRELA and XPHOZAH were scheduled to expire in August 2033 and April 2034, respectively. As a result, the market has been reducing the value of the terminal value due to the impending loss of exclusivity. With the recent extension of protection through 2041, it essentially extends the peak or near-peak cash flow of the company by seven to eight years. Given that Ardelyx estimates IBSRELA to be at $1 billion in annual revenue by 2029, extending that revenue stream by ten years past the original cliff creates huge incremental value that I feel is not currently being represented in the stock price. This extension converts IBSRELA into a generational asset for the company and increases the attractiveness of the company as an acquisition target because any potential acquirer would now have a monopoly on the formulation for fifteen years.

Counterarguments and Risk Assessment

One major concern for Ardelyx may be the slow growth trajectory for XPHOZAH. Although Ardelyx has generated $104 million in revenue for the full year 2025, the growth rate of XPHOZAH is significantly lower than that of IBSRELA. Due to the use of a bundled payment system in dialysis and the inherent complexity of accessing patients (both in terms of patient flow through the clinic and regulatory compliance), the renal market has historically been very challenging to penetrate. With a revenue projection of $110 to $120 million by 2026 for XPHOZAH, Ardelyx would be implying a CAGR for XPHOZAH of approximately 6% to 15%. In stark comparison, the CAGR for IBSRELA is anticipated to be approximately 50%. Should the renal franchise experience difficulties either penetrating the market or experiencing increased reimbursement challenges, the company's valuation multiple for its equity could potentially suffer, leading to investors viewing Ardelyx as a GI company with a management team distracted by the renal franchise.

In addition to facing competitive challenges in the GI space with respect to the growth of IBSRELA, Ardelyx will face a significant challenge in achieving its $1 billion revenue goal for IBSRELA by 2029. In order to achieve this goal, IBSRELA will need to continue to displace cheap generic drugs and competing branded agents, and therefore, there exists an execution risk with respect to Ardelyx's ability to achieve this goal. Specifically, should the sales force for IBSRELA reach a saturation point with the early adopter base and fail to penetrate the mass market of general prescribers, the growth curve for IBSRELA may flatten out faster than implied by the 2029 target date.

Ardelyx's dependence upon a single mechanism of action—NHE3 inhibition—is a biological risk that represents a concentration of biological risk across all three products that are currently being developed. At this time, the safety profile for this mechanism of action is well understood; however, any emergence of long-term side effects associated with NHE3 inhibition could have the potential to jeopardize both the renal and GI space outlooks for the company.

Market Opportunity and Competitive Landscape

In the United States, IBS-C indicated net product sales of approximately $4.0 billion occurred in 2024. Growth within the market has also continued. Prescription volumes grew by 21% between 2020 and 2024. Patient satisfaction, however, is where the unique mechanism of action of IBSRELA represents a competitive barrier to entry. Only approximately 23% of the patients using prescription treatment for IBS-C do not continue to experience residual symptoms. Therefore, this is where the unique mechanism of action of IBSRELA represents a competitive barrier to entry compared to other products such as Linzess and Trulance, which act by stimulating fluid secretion via the guanylate cyclase-C receptor (a secretagogue). In contrast, IBSRELA reduces sodium absorption via local inhibition of the NHE3 transporter. As a result, this is a fundamentally different product design. In a market where patients continually try new products until finding relief, IBSRELA is able to attract those who have previously tried Linzess or Trulance and found them to be ineffective.

In terms of the renal indication, Ardelyx estimates the market size for hyperphosphatemia is over 550,000 patients on dialysis in the U.S. alone. The standard of care has traditionally been phosphate-binding agents. These agents require patients to take a high dose of pills with each meal to bind phosphorus prior to absorption. The company estimated that roughly 70% of the patients on phosphate binders are unable to keep their phosphorus levels at target. XPHOZAH does not compete as simply another phosphate binder in this space. Instead, it competes as a blocking agent that prevents absorption of phosphorus. While the generic phosphate binders may be inexpensive, there exists a ceiling effect on their efficacy, allowing XPHOZAH to be marketed as a complementary or alternative therapy for those patients who have experienced inadequate response to existing therapies.

The competitive environment in this space is somewhat less about competing with other branded drugs and more about the clinical inertia associated with traditional practices and the price sensitivity of dialysis providers. Nevertheless, XPHOZAH was able to obtain greater than $100 million in revenue during its first full year of commercialization, suggesting that nephrologists would be interested in a therapeutic agent that functions in a manner that is significantly different than the calcium or sevelamer-based binders that have been used for many years.

Future Outlook and Conclusion

I believe the market has yet to fully factor in the implications of the patent extension and the durability of the IBSRELA growth curve. Many investors look for the next big thing in biotechnology and miss the compounding value of a successful commercial launch that is currently underway before them. Ardelyx is no longer a speculative story concerning what may happen at the FDA; it is now a story about executing on the sales process and penetrating the marketplace. As such, I believe that Ardelyx presents a compelling risk-reward profile for investors that are willing to see the company through the expansionary phase of its commercial development.
